pet.name: Saw
pet.special_needs: Needs more oxygen than other fishes..
pet.characteristics: High-oxygen fish typically include active species such as goldfish, plecos, or fast-swimming cyprinids that require robust aeration and water circulation. Care involves maintaining dissolved oxygen levels through air stones, powerheads, or surface agitation; regular water changes (25–50% weekly) to prevent oxygen depletion; cooler water temperatures when possible, as cold water holds more oxygen; and avoiding overcrowding. Monitor for gasping at the surface, lethargy, or rapid gill movement—signs of hypoxia. Diet should be species-appropriate (flakes, pellets, or vegetables depending on type) offered once or twice daily in amounts consumed within 2–3 minutes. Tank size should accommodate activity level and bioload; enrichment includes plants, hiding spots, and open swimming space. Handling is generally not recommended; observe fish behavior and water parameters (pH, ammonia, nitrite, nitrate) regularly.
pet.species_profile_summary: Lifespan varies widely by specific species (3–10 years typical for common high-oxygen fish in captive care); success depends on consistent aeration, water quality, and appropriate tank size.

age_context.summary: Lifespan varies widely by specific species (3–10 years typical for common high-oxygen fish in captive care); success depends on consistent aeration, water quality, and appropriate tank size.

goal: Create a complete pet care plan with daily, weekly, monthly tasks, reminders, and safe condition-aware guidance.

retrieved_passages.title: Medication Routine and Safety
retrieved_passages.content: Medication-related tasks in a home routine should focus on reminders, documentation, and observation, not dosage changes. A planning assistant can remind the owner to give medication exactly as already prescribed and to log whether the dose was completed.

retrieved_passages.title: Senior Pet Support
retrieved_passages.content: A senior pet plan should prioritize consistency over intensity. When time is limited, essential care such as feeding, hydration, medication reminders, and mobility support should be scheduled before optional enrichment.

retrieved_passages.title: Enrichment and Daily Monitoring
retrieved_passages.content: Observation tasks are useful for special-needs pets because they create a record of appetite, water intake, energy, and bathroom habits. These tasks should be short, specific, and easy to complete consistently.

retrieved_passages.title: Kidney Diet and Hydration Support
retrieved_passages.content: Owners should follow the veterinary feeding plan already prescribed and avoid changing food or supplement amounts without veterinary guidance. If a pet refuses multiple meals or shows sudden decline, the safe action is to contact a veterinarian.

retrieved_passages.title: Senior Pet Support
retrieved_passages.content: Older pets may benefit from routines that include medication reminders, slow feeding, and extra time for transitions. Owners should watch for changes in gait, confusion, appetite, and bathroom habits and report major changes to a veterinarian.

raw_recommendations.name: Feed Saw once daily
raw_recommendations.notes: Offer species-appropriate food (flakes, pellets, or vegetables) in amounts consumed within 2–3 minutes. Remove uneaten food to maintain water quality.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Consistent feeding supports senior fish health and prevents water quality degradation from excess food.

raw_recommendations.name: Monitor Saw's behavior and breathing
raw_recommendations.notes: Observe for gasping at the surface, lethargy, or rapid gill movement—signs of low oxygen. Note energy level and appetite. Keep a brief log.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Saw requires more oxygen than typical fish. Daily observation creates a record of appetite and energy, essential for detecting hypoxia or decline early.

raw_recommendations.name: Check aeration and water circulation
raw_recommendations.notes: Verify air stone, powerhead, or surface agitation is functioning. Ensure dissolved oxygen levels are adequate for Saw's high-oxygen needs.
raw_recommendations.rationale: High-oxygen fish depend on robust aeration. Daily checks prevent equipment failure that could cause hypoxia.

raw_recommendations.name: Perform 25–50% water change
raw_recommendations.notes: Change 25–50% of tank water weekly to prevent oxygen depletion and maintain water parameters (pH, ammonia, nitrite, nitrate).
raw_recommendations.rationale: Regular water changes are critical for high-oxygen fish to sustain dissolved oxygen and remove waste that depletes oxygen.

raw_recommendations.name: Test water parameters
raw_recommendations.notes: Check pH, ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate levels weekly. Record results to track trends.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Monitoring water parameters helps detect conditions that reduce oxygen availability and supports early intervention.

raw_recommendations.name: Verify tank temperature and oxygen capacity
raw_recommendations.notes: Keep water as cool as species tolerates; cooler water holds more dissolved oxygen. Check thermometer weekly.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Temperature management is a key strategy for high-oxygen fish. Cooler water naturally supports higher dissolved oxygen levels.

raw_recommendations.name: Inspect tank for overcrowding and bioload
raw_recommendations.notes: Ensure tank size accommodates Saw's activity level and bioload. Avoid overcrowding, which depletes oxygen. Verify hiding spots and open swimming space are available.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Tank size and stocking density directly affect oxygen availability. Monthly review ensures the environment remains suitable for a high-oxygen fish.

raw_recommendations.name: Review Saw's health log and report major changes
raw_recommendations.notes: Review observations from the past month. If Saw shows sudden decline, refusal of multiple meals, or persistent gasping, contact a veterinarian.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Senior fish benefit from consistency and early detection of problems. A monthly review supports proactive care and safe escalation to veterinary guidance when needed.
